claim: Calling a panicked or loose dog is counterproductive because if too many people have already tried to capture it, 'calling' becomes a conditioned trigger that causes the dog to bolt from anyone — including its owner.
stance_target: Do-not-call protocol for lost dogs in flight/survival mode
scope_conditions: Dogs that have already been chased or approached multiple times; does not apply to dogs that have not yet entered flight mode
quoted_support: One of the worst things that you can do is CALL a stray, loose, or panicked dog. That's because if too many people have already tried to capture the dog, calling him becomes a 'trigger' that can cause him to automatically take off in fear when anyone, including his owner, calls him.

claim: Xenophobic (fearful) dogs travel farther than other temperament types when lost, are at higher risk of car strikes, and may run even from their own owners — requiring trap-based or magnet-dog capture rather than pursuit.
stance_target: Temperament-differentiated search strategy — xenophobic dogs require specialized non-pursuit recovery protocols
scope_conditions: Dogs with genetic or puppyhood-conditioned fear/xenophobic temperament
quoted_support: Dogs with xenophobic temperaments (due to genetics and/or puppyhood experiences) are more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars. Due to their cowering, fearful behavior, people assume these dogs were 'abused,' and even if the dog has ID tags, they will refuse to contact the previous owner. Some of these panic-stricken dogs will even run from their owners!

claim: Blind panic — triggered by xenophobic temperament, loud noises (thunder/fireworks/gunfire), or traumatic incidents — causes dogs to run at high speed for several miles and then hide from all human contact, making them the hardest category to recover.
stance_target: Phase-0 behavioral triage: dogs lost in panic require fundamentally different protocol from dogs lost by wanderlust or opportunistic journey
scope_conditions: Panic-mode dogs; onset depends on triggering event
quoted_support: Blind panic is a situation in which the 'flight' instinct (from the hardwired 'fight or flight' response to stimuli) kicks in and a dog runs in what we call a blind panic. This can happen for three reasons: xenophobic (skittish) temperament, loud noises (thunder, gunfire), or traumatic incident (involved in car accident, explosion, etc). These dogs are the most difficult to catch since they will travel far, travel fast, and avoid human contact, even with their own family members!

claim: In the majority of lost dog cases, searchers are effectively looking for people — humans who picked up or witnessed the dog — because loose dogs prompt human intervention at much higher rates than loose cats.
stance_target: Witness-network canvassing and social media broadcast as primary early-phase recovery tactic
scope_conditions: Urban and suburban environments with significant human density
quoted_support: This means that in the majority of lost dog cases, you are actually looking for people — people who found your dog (and have him in their possession) or people who have seen your dog (and know the area where you should be searching).

claim: Three behavioral categories of lost dogs — Gregarious, Aloof, and Xenophobic — predict different travel distances, recovery timelines, and required capture methods.
stance_target: Behavioral-profile-parameterized search radius and strategy selection
scope_conditions: General; applies across breeds and environments
quoted_support: There are three primary behavioral categories of lost dogs: Gregarious Dogs, Aloof Dogs, and Xenophobic Dogs. [Gregarious] will generally be found fairly close to home... [Aloof] eventually can be enticed with food and patience, typically by experienced rescuers... [Xenophobic] are more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars.

claim: Six factors determine how far a lost dog travels: temperament, circumstances of disappearance (exploration vs. panic bolt), weather, terrain (barriers/path-of-least-resistance), appearance (breed perception affecting human intervention probability), and population density.
stance_target: Multi-factor search radius model for lost dogs
quoted_support: There are six major factors that influence the distances that a lost dog will travel: Temperament, Circumstances, Weather, Terrain, Appearance, and Population Density.

claim: Wanderlust — the drive to actively escape and roam — is especially prevalent in intact male dogs and hound breeds, causing them to climb, dig, or bolt whenever the opportunity arises.
stance_target: Breed-specific behavioral priors for podenco/galgo/hound breed protocols
scope_conditions: Intact male dogs and hound breed categories
quoted_support: Wanderlust is a common problem in intact male dogs of any breed as well as certain breeds like hounds. These dogs will actively attempt to escape by climbing, digging, or wiggling to escape their yards.

claim: Owner error patterns that reduce recovery probability include: 'wait and see' delay (missing critical first hours), tunnel-vision theorizing (e.g. assuming theft), and grief avoidance leading to premature search abandonment.
scope_conditions: Owner behavior in first hours/days after loss
quoted_support: Some develop a 'wait and see' approach (believing their dog will return home like Lassie) and by the time they start actively looking, the vital first few hours to locate the dog (or witnesses who saw the dog) are gone. Others develop 'tunnel vision'... They experience 'grief avoidance' and quickly give up their search effort.

claim: People who find stray dogs systematically misinterpret xenophobic behavior as evidence of abuse, and take dogs found in rural areas to be dumped strays rather than lost pets, both errors reducing the probability the dog is returned to its owner.
stance_target: Public education and finder-behavior error prevention in lost dog recovery
scope_conditions: Rural and suburban finder behavior; xenophobic temperament dogs
quoted_support: People who find stray dogs often misinterpret the dog's behavior; they assume that the cowering, fearful dog was 'abused' when in fact the dog has a xenophobic temperament and has been shy and fearful since it was a puppy, due to genetics and puppyhood experiences. Dogs found in rural areas are often assumed to be 'dumped' and homeless.
